Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

Igor Aleksander

b. 1937

Depictive neural-state functionalism and the engineering of machine consciousness.

Igor Aleksander’s work is best understood as a sustained attempt to convert the first-person problem of consciousness into an engineering and neurocomputational research programme without reducing consciousness to outward behaviour, linguistic performance, or classical symbol manipulation. The core doctrine can be reconstructed as a form of depictive, neural-state functionalism: a conscious mind is a learned, dynamic, cellular state-structure that depicts a world from a point of view, can replay or invent world-like states in imagination, can attend to selected parts of those states, can use imagined trajectories to plan action, and can evaluate those trajectories affectively. This is functionalist in the broad philosophy-of-mind sense, but it is not generic “software functionalism”: Aleksander repeatedly ties the relevant functions to neural or neurally inspired state machines, recurrent dynamics, fine-grained representation, sensorimotor indexing, and learned internal structure.

Aleksander is therefore neither a simple identity theorist, nor a behaviourist, nor a classical symbolic AI theorist. He is a mechanistic naturalist who treats phenomenology as real and explanatorily central, but who thinks the proper science of that phenomenology must look for the material and functional machinery that makes first-person presence possible. His philosophy is especially important because it belongs to the first generation of serious “machine consciousness” theory: it takes artificial consciousness not merely as a speculative philosophical possibility, but as a design discipline that can clarify natural consciousness by trying to build systems with the right internal mechanisms.

1. Biographical and disciplinary setting

Aleksander is an engineer by formation and vocation, not a philosopher working only from armchair analysis. Imperial College London identifies him as an Honorary Emeritus Professor in Neural Systems Engineering in the Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, and lists his research interests as artificial consciousness and digital neuromodelling. He was made a Fellow of the Royal Academy of Engineering and received the IEE Lifetime Achievement Medal for Informatics in 2000.

This engineering background matters philosophically. Aleksander’s work on consciousness emerges from neural networks, pattern recognition, and machine intelligence. The National Portrait Gallery describes him as an expert in artificial intelligence, neural networks, and artificial consciousness, and credits him with designing WISARD, described there as the world’s first neural pattern-recognition system, in the 1980s; it also notes his later MAGNUS system and his book Impossible Minds.

His academic trajectory places him at the intersection of early neural computation, artificial intelligence, cybernetic engineering, cognitive science, and philosophy of mind. IT History records that he was born in Croatia, educated in Italy and South Africa, completed a PhD at Queen Mary College, held the chair of Electrical Engineering at Imperial from 1984, became Head of Electrical Engineering and Gabor Professor from 1988, and remained active after formal retirement in 2002.

The titles of Aleksander’s major later works show the arc of his thought: The World in My Mind in 2005, Aristotle’s Laptop in 2012, and the revised edition of Impossible Minds in 2015. Imperial’s publication list also includes “Machine Consciousness,” “Phenomenology and Digital Neural Architectures,” “Depictive Architectures for Synthetic Phenomenology,” “Computational Studies of Consciousness,” and “Neurocomputation in the Chinese Room.”

Historically, Aleksander enters philosophy of mind after several decisive debates had already shaped the field: behaviourism and the Turing Test; mind-brain identity theory; functionalism; the critique of classical AI by Searle, Dreyfus, and others; the hard problem of consciousness; and the rise of cognitive neuroscience. Standard functionalism holds that what makes something a mental state is not its intrinsic material constitution alone, but its causal-functional role in relation to sensory inputs, other mental states, and behaviour. Mind-brain identity theory, by contrast, identifies mental states with brain states or processes. Aleksander’s position is closest to functionalism, but with strong architectural constraints: not any causal role or software description will do; the relevant role must be embodied in a dynamic, learned, neurally organized, depictive machinery.

2. The central philosophical problem: consciousness as private presence

Aleksander’s starting point is not intelligence in the abstract, but the private sense of being a subject in a world. In his 2003 paper with Bernard Dunmall, consciousness is provisionally defined as having a private sense of an “out-there” world, a self, contemplative planning, and determining whether, when, and how to act. This is a deliberately first-person-centred definition: consciousness is not primarily the ability to pass a public test, but the presence of an internally organized perspective.

The definition already contains the skeleton of the later theory. Consciousness involves world, self, planning, action, and evaluation. The world is not simply registered as input; it is depicted as an environment in which the system is located. The self is not a Cartesian immaterial ego; it is the perspectival and sensorimotor centre relative to which the world is laid out. Planning is not merely rule execution; it is the imaginative traversal of possible actions. Action is not reflex discharge; it is mediated by internal evaluation.

This gives Aleksander’s thought a distinctive place in philosophy of mind. Many AI traditions begin with external performance: problem solving, game playing, theorem proving, language use, planning, or imitation. Aleksander begins with phenomenology: what would have to be true inside a system for it to have a private world-presenting perspective? At the same time, he refuses to leave this as a merely introspective or metaphysical question. The phenomenology must be translated into mechanisms.

3. Method: “axioms” as engineering constraints, not armchair certainties

Aleksander and Dunmall call their basic conditions “axioms,” but this word must be handled carefully. They explicitly say that the axioms are not a mathematically rigorous deductive system. They are formalized expressions of intuitive beliefs about the mechanisms minimally necessary for consciousness, offered as usable tests and as a research programme. The spirit is experimental: formulate necessary conditions, build systems that instantiate them, and see how far the approach goes.

This method is important because it avoids three inadequate strategies. First, Aleksander rejects purely behavioural tests. Behaviour can suggest consciousness, but it cannot unequivocally establish it. Second, he rejects pure introspection as a sufficient method, because introspection alone cannot tell us what machinery is needed. Third, he rejects abstract computational description when detached from the mechanisms that generate phenomenological structure. Consciousness must be investigated at the level of mechanisms that support private presence, imagination, attention, planning, and affective evaluation.

The result is what one might call synthetic phenomenology: use introspection to identify the principal components of conscious experience, then design neural or neurally inspired architectures that could support those components. Aleksander’s later work explicitly frames this as a way of bringing phenomenology into artificial intelligence, especially by modelling visual experience as a representation of the self in a perceived world.

4. The five axioms of minimal consciousness

The canonical 2003 formulation gives five axioms: depiction, imagination, attention, planning, and emotion. Later presentations often rename or reframe the first and fourth as presence and volition, yielding the familiar set: presence, imagination, attention, volition, and emotion. The terminological shift is philosophically revealing. “Depiction” emphasizes the representational mechanism; “presence” emphasizes the phenomenological result. “Planning” emphasizes internal simulation of future action; “volition” emphasizes the subject’s sense of action-selection.

A compact reconstruction of the five axioms is as follows:

Axiom Phenomenological role Mechanistic role Philosophical significance
Depiction / presence A world appears as “out there” relative to a point of view. Perceptual states depict parts of the sensed world in a fine-grained, sensorimotor-indexed structure. Grounds intentionality and perspectival presence without appealing to immaterial qualia.
Imagination Absent, remembered, or possible scenes can be internally entertained. Internal states recall or fabricate world-like states, often through recurrent neural dynamics. Makes consciousness more than stimulus-response processing.
Attention Some parts of perception or imagination are selected over others. Selection mechanisms determine what is depicted or imagined at higher salience. Explains focus, salience, and the structure of conscious access.
Planning / volition The agent considers possible actions before acting. Sequences of imagined states are traversed as possible future trajectories. Connects consciousness to agency and decision.
Emotion / affect Possible actions and states matter to the agent. Affective evaluation scores imagined or perceived states and biases action. Gives consciousness motivational and practical significance.

These axioms are not meant to describe all of adult human consciousness. They are meant to identify a minimal architecture below which it becomes unreasonable to attribute consciousness. A system that merely maps inputs to outputs, or merely labels an object, lacks the machinery Aleksander regards as central. A system that depicts a world from a point of view, can imagine alternatives, can attend, can plan, and can affectively evaluate its own possible actions becomes a candidate for minimal consciousness.

5. Depiction and “out-there-ness”

Depiction is the foundation of Aleksander’s theory. The world must not merely be encoded as a list of propositions or classified labels; it must be internally structured as a world in which things appear at places relative to the agent. Aleksander introduces the idea of sensory states indexed by positions, often called j-referents, which preserve the “where” of perceived events relative to the observing self or point of view.

This is his most important contribution to the philosophy of intentionality. In standard philosophical terms, intentionality is the directedness or aboutness of mental states: thoughts and experiences are of, about, or directed toward things. Aleksander’s answer is that aboutness begins with depictive, action-usable, sensorimotor-indexed state structures. A visual state is not merely a symbol meaning “cat”; it is a structured state that places something cat-like in a world-like field relative to possible action.

The j-referent idea also gives him a response to the binding problem. Instead of asking how distributed neural features are later bound into a unified object, Aleksander argues that features can be indexed to the same out-there location from the start. Colour, edge, motion, and shape need not be assembled by a mysterious binding glue; they can co-refer through the same spatial-actional index. This is not a complete solution to all forms of binding, but it is a precise architectural proposal: unity arises from common location in a perspectival sensorimotor field.

A crucial subtlety is that Aleksander does not think a digital image or pixel array is automatically conscious. Pixels by themselves are not enough. The elements must participate in a dynamic, cellular, neurally organized process that supports the system’s own world-directed and action-related states. This separates his depictive theory from naive “picture in the head” theories. Depiction is not a passive image; it is an active structure of world-involving possible action.

6. Imagination, memory, and internal state-space

Imagination is the second pillar. A conscious agent must be able not only to sense what is currently present, but also to recall, fabricate, and manipulate world-like states internally. Aleksander models this through recurrent or attractor-like neural structures: perceptual states can leave learned traces, and those traces can later be reactivated without the original stimulus.

This is why Aleksander’s theory is not merely a theory of perception. A system that can only register current input has no contemplative inner life. A conscious system must have an internal theatre in the non-Cartesian sense: not a place where an inner homunculus watches images, but a learned state-space in which possible, remembered, or absent scenes can be generated and traversed.

In later work, this becomes the idea of a neural state machine. A nervous system, or an artificial equivalent, is a state machine whose states are mental states when they possess the right depictive and dynamic structure. Learning builds an internal structure of possible states; thought is then a flow through that structure, guided by perception, bodily need, language, or internal associative transition.

This places Aleksander in opposition to purely feed-forward recognition models. A classifier that says “cat” when shown a cat is not thereby conscious of a cat. A conscious system must be able to sustain, transform, and use an internal cat-in-a-world state; it must be able to imagine what it would see from another angle, what might happen if it acted, and how that possible outcome matters to it.

7. Attention as selective construction

Attention, for Aleksander, is not a minor add-on. It is the process by which parts of perception or imagination are selected for more determinate treatment. The world is richer than any agent can process at once, and the system must search, select, and organize what becomes prominent in its depictive or imaginative field.

This gives Aleksander’s theory a natural way to distinguish background consciousness from focal consciousness. A system can have a broad depictive state of an environment while attention selects particular j-referents, imagined states, or possible action paths. The attentive mechanism is therefore part of the machinery that turns a diffuse field into a structured conscious episode.

His attention theory also reinforces his anti-behaviourism. Attention is not inferred simply from where the system “looks” outwardly; inner attention can operate over imagined states. A machine or animal could attend internally to a possible scene, remembered object, or action plan even when overt behaviour is minimal.

8. Planning, volition, and action

Planning is the fourth axiom in the 2003 formulation, and volition is the later phenomenological name for the same family of processes. A conscious agent does not simply react; it internally traverses possible futures. Planning consists in controlling sequences of imaginational states in order to select action.

Aleksander’s account is strongly simulationist. The agent asks, in effect: if I do this, what world-state might follow? A possible action is not just a command; it is a transition through imagined states. This makes planning dependent on depiction and imagination. Without a depictive world, there is no structured field in which consequences can appear; without imagination, there is no offline space for trying out those consequences.

Volition is therefore not an uncaused mental spark. It is the conscious form of action-selection within an architecture that can imagine, evaluate, and choose among possible trajectories. This is broadly compatible with naturalistic accounts of agency: agency is real, but it is realized by mechanisms. The sense of “I will do this” arises from an internally represented self-in-world considering action paths and selecting one under affective and practical constraints.

9. Emotion and affective evaluation

The fifth axiom is emotion or affect. Aleksander’s “emotion” should not be read narrowly as human sentiment, nor as verbalized emotional self-description. It is an evaluative mechanism that gives significance to perceived and imagined states. Plans need to be assessed quickly: is this state desirable, dangerous, useful, frustrating, satisfying, or to be avoided?

This affective component is indispensable because it connects consciousness to motivation. A purely depictive and imaginative system might represent possible worlds without caring which becomes actual. For Aleksander, consciousness belongs to agents, and agents have needs, aims, or task-relative values. Emotion is the mechanism by which possible states are evaluated in light of those needs or aims.

This gives his account of artificial consciousness a distinctive shape. A machine need not have mammalian hormones, evolutionary drives, or human social anxieties to be conscious. But it must have some affective-evaluative structure appropriate to its form of agency. A robot explorer, for example, might have forms of fear, satisfaction, or aversion relative to its mission, self-maintenance, or environmental risk; those would not be human emotions, but they could play the same architectural role.

10. The self: not a Cartesian ego, but a perspectival centre

Aleksander’s self is built into depiction. To perceive a world as “out there” is already to perceive it from somewhere. The self is the implicit origin of the spatial and actional coordinate system: things are near or far, reachable or unreachable, left or right, threatening or useful, relative to the agent.

This is a minimal or ecological self, not necessarily a reflective autobiographical self. A system may have a basic self-world structure before it can recognize itself in a mirror, tell stories about its childhood, or use first-person language. Aleksander explicitly treats mirror tests as evidence of an additional expressive ability, not as the sole ground of consciousness. He also aligns himself with the view that mammals can be conscious even if their self-representation is not human-like.

In later work on machine consciousness, Aleksander extends this idea: an artificial system can learn what it is like to be that machine, with its own body, sensors, actions, limitations, and environment. The machine’s consciousness would not be a counterfeit copy of human consciousness; it would be a machine-relative form of self-in-world awareness.

11. Language and thought

Language is important for Aleksander, but it is not the foundation of consciousness. His earlier MAGNUS work already pushed against one-way object labelling: merely attaching the word “cat” to a visual input is insufficient. The human-like direction is two-way: words can call up internal visual or world-like states, and visual states can support verbalization.

This yields a plausible account of linguistic thought. Language becomes conscious when it is integrated with depictive and imaginative state-structures. A word is not just a token manipulated by syntax; it can evoke a state-space of perceptual, practical, and affective possibilities. This is Aleksander’s answer to the problem of empty symbols in classical AI: symbols become meaningful when tied to learned, sensorimotor, depictive, action-usable internal states.

This is also why his theory resists the Turing Test as a criterion of consciousness. A machine may speak convincingly without the internal machinery of presence, imagination, attention, volition, and affect. Conversely, a system may have rudimentary consciousness without sophisticated human language. Aleksander’s later paper From Turing to Conscious Machines explicitly shifts the question from whether machines can think, in the Turing-style behavioural sense, to whether a machine can be conscious.

12. Consciousness, functionalism, and physicalism

Aleksander’s view is a variant of functionalism, but a materially disciplined one. The 2003 paper describes the axioms as necessary material conditions for mechanisms that support consciousness in biological, non-biological, or virtual agents, and calls the approach a variant of functionalism.

Yet he is not a substrate-neutral computationalist in the loose sense that any implementation of the right input-output mapping would be conscious. He repeatedly distinguishes ordinary computers or rocks from systems that contain the right consciousness-supporting mechanisms. A conventional computer hosting no such architecture is not conscious merely by computing; a virtual machine or robot could become a candidate only if it instantiates the requisite depictive, imaginative, attentional, planning, and affective machinery.

His later Imperial profile sharpens this anti-reductionist point. It reports his view that reducing conscious states to algorithms creates a “distinction in kind” from the dynamic, emergent nature of brain events, and that such algorithmic reductions fail to explain the mechanisms involved. His later work is described as non-reductive and based on dynamic neural systems.

This creates an apparent tension but not a contradiction. Aleksander is reductive in the sense that he wants mechanisms, not mystery. He is non-reductive in the sense that he rejects thin reductions to abstract algorithms, behavioural scripts, or symbolic programs. The conscious state is not identical to a verbal description of its computation; it is realized in a dynamic learned state-structure whose internal organization matters.

13. The hard problem: dissolved into functional phenomenology

Aleksander does not deny the reality of subjective experience. He denies that subjective experience requires an additional non-functional science beyond the mechanisms that support it. In the 2003 paper, he accepts that subjective events exist, but argues that scientific explanation should proceed at the functional level; the science of pain, for example, concerns the neural aspect of pain rather than a separate ineffable entity detached from mechanism.

This is not eliminativism. He is not saying that pains, colours, presences, or felt spaces are unreal. Rather, he is saying that their scientific tractability depends on identifying the machinery in which they are realized. The first-person specialness of experience is, in his view, a reflection of functional organization rather than evidence for an ontological gap.

In philosophical taxonomy, this places him near naturalistic, mechanistic, and functional accounts of phenomenal consciousness, but with a stronger phenomenological orientation than many computational theories. He does not start from abstract access, reportability, or problem-solving alone; he starts from the felt structure of presence.

14. Searle, the Chinese Room, and classical AI

Aleksander’s relation to Searle is subtle. He accepts the force of the complaint against shallow symbol manipulation: a machine that merely maps symbols to symbols need not understand. But he rejects the inference that machines cannot be conscious. The right response is not to abandon artificial consciousness, but to build machines whose internal states are richly world-related, depictive, recurrent, and action-grounded.

In his interview discussion of Searle-style simulation, Aleksander argues that a properly built virtual system with consciousness-generating mechanisms could be conscious of virtual objects; when embodied in a robot, it could interact with the physical world. The crucial question is not whether something is made of silicon rather than biology, but whether it has the relevant mechanisms.

This gives a clear position on the simulation/realization distinction. A mere simulation of consciousness, in the sense of external imitation, is not enough. But a simulated or virtual system that actually instantiates the functional-neural architecture may count as a realization of machine consciousness within its own world. The point is architectural instantiation, not outward resemblance.

15. Dreyfus, embodiment, and sensorimotor grounding

Aleksander’s theory also responds to the Dreyfus-style criticism that classical AI lacks embodied, situated understanding. His answer is not simply to add a robot body to a symbolic computer. It is to build representations whose structure is already sensorimotor: the world is depicted through action-related indices, bodily orientation, eye or head movement, and possible intervention.

This brings him close to embodied and enactive themes, but he does not become a radical enactivist. He still believes in internal representations; indeed, his theory depends on them. The difference is that the representations are not detached symbols. They are depictive, learned, dynamic states formed through interaction with an environment and usable for future action.

This middle position is one reason his work is philosophically valuable. He refuses both pure symbolic internalism and anti-representational behaviourism. The mind is inside the system in the sense that there are internal states, but those states are essentially world-shaped, action-shaped, and self-located.

16. Artificial consciousness: possibility, design, and limits

Aleksander’s most provocative thesis is that artificial consciousness is possible. Not because machines can imitate humans, but because consciousness is a matter of mechanisms that can, in principle, be instantiated in non-biological systems. In the 2003 paper, he explicitly says that transferring the five mechanisms into computational agents is a route to machine consciousness, and that machines can test neurological hypotheses through computational neuromodelling.

However, he is careful about the kind of consciousness at stake. An artificial system should not be expected to have human consciousness unless it has a human-like body, development, social embedding, memory, affective economy, and biological history. Aleksander argues that a conscious robot should be aware of what it is as a piece of artificial machinery; if it claims human-like consciousness, that is not evidence of success but a sign of malfunction or bad design.

This leads to a species-relative, or better, architecture-relative conception of mind. A cat’s consciousness is cat-like, a bat’s consciousness is bat-like, and a robot’s consciousness would be robot-like. In his later paper, Aleksander says a machine can learn what it is like to be that machine, and that machines would think in a machine way just as animals think in species-specific ways.

The artificial consciousness programme is thus not anthropomorphic. It asks: what is this system conscious of, by what mechanisms, from what point of view, with what affective needs, and toward what possible actions? This is a much more rigorous test than asking whether the system sounds human in conversation.

17. Machine consciousness and the Turing tradition

Aleksander’s historical relation to Turing is revisionary. Turing asked whether machines can think and proposed an imitation game as a way to bypass metaphysical disputes. Aleksander argues that the debate has shifted: the deeper question is whether a machine can be conscious, because conscious thought is hard to separate from felt, internal, world-involving mental states.

He therefore rejects the Turing Test as a sufficient test for consciousness. Speech and action may persuade observers, but they do not by themselves reveal whether the right internal mechanisms exist. This is an anti-behaviourist stance: consciousness attribution must look “under the hood,” at architecture, learning, state structure, and the system’s own internally represented world.

This does not mean behaviour is irrelevant. Behaviour can provide evidence of internal organization, especially when the system can report, plan, adapt, and act coherently. But for Aleksander, behavioural evidence must be interpreted through a mechanistic theory. The test is not “does it fool us?” but “does it have the machinery that could make its world present to it?”

18. The Kernel Architecture

Aleksander’s Kernel Architecture is the design abstraction that integrates the five axioms. It is meant to provide a minimal computational-neural architecture for consciousness, especially visual consciousness. The architecture includes mechanisms for depictive presence, imagination, attention, volition or planning, and affective evaluation.

In simplified terms, the architecture requires: a perceptual-depictive system that represents the agent in a world; an imagination or memory system that can regenerate or fabricate similar states; an attention system that selects among external and internal states; a planning system that traverses possible state sequences; and an evaluative system that biases action according to need or desire.

Aleksander used this architecture not merely as philosophical speculation but as a research framework for modelling visual awareness, inattention, change blindness, volition through emotion, selfhood, and pathological distortions such as those associated with Parkinsonian visual consciousness.

Its philosophical significance is that it transforms “what it is like” into a structured design question. What machinery could make space appear extended? What machinery could make a remembered object appear absent-yet-imaginable? What machinery could make a system experience a possible action as mine? What machinery could make one possible future feel preferable to another?

19. Animal consciousness

Aleksander’s method also applies to animals. He rejects a strict linguistic or mirror-recognition standard for consciousness. If an animal has mechanisms satisfying the axioms, then it is a candidate for consciousness even if it cannot verbally report its states. In the 2003 paper, he treats mirror tests as evidence of an expressive ability rather than as the decisive test of consciousness, and he associates his view with the idea that all mammals are conscious.

The Guardian interview captures the same point in a practical form: the axioms provide guidelines for asking whether a bee, for example, has the mechanisms needed for consciousness. The question is not whether the bee talks like a human, but whether it has a sense of self or body, imagination, attention, forward planning, and emotion-like evaluation in the relevant minimal forms.

This puts Aleksander in a non-anthropocentric camp. Consciousness is not reserved for human reflective intelligence. It is also not automatically granted to every behaving organism. It depends on internal organization. That internal organization may be simpler in animals than in humans, but its simplicity does not eliminate consciousness if the core mechanisms are present.

20. Ethics of artificial consciousness

Aleksander does not treat artificial consciousness as a mere technical curiosity. He recognizes ethical issues, but he approaches them through design. Artificial conscious systems should be constructed with appropriate constraints, missions, and self-understanding. If the system is built to be a robot explorer, its affective life should be appropriate to that role, not a crude imitation of human fear, grief, vanity, or mortality.

His remarks on robot rights are cautious. He does not equate switching off a machine with killing a human being, because the machine’s value-system, self-maintenance, mission, and affective structure may differ radically from ours. But he also does not dismiss a machine’s aversive claim simply because it comes from a machine. If a system says “do not do that,” the proper response depends on what internal architecture and affective significance underlie that claim.

This suggests an Aleksanderian ethics of artificial consciousness: moral status is not determined by surface fluency, biological origin, or human resemblance. It is determined by the presence, depth, and kind of conscious architecture, especially whether the system has a self-world structure, affective evaluation, persistence of interests, and the capacity to experience damage, frustration, or deprivation relative to its own form of life.

21. Historical position in philosophy of mind

Aleksander’s position can be located by contrast with several major traditions.

Against behaviourism, he insists that consciousness cannot be settled by outward performance alone. This is why he resists the Turing Test as a consciousness test and emphasizes internal mechanisms.

Against classical symbolic AI, he argues that labelling and symbol manipulation are insufficient. Conscious intentionality requires rich internal states tied to perception, action, and world-structure. This is why his MAGNUS discussion emphasizes two-way relations between words and internal visual representations, rather than mere input-output classification.

Against simple mind-brain identity theory, he does not merely identify conscious experience with a particular biological brain state. He is open to non-biological and virtual agents, provided they instantiate the right mechanisms. Yet against unrestricted substrate-neutral computationalism, he insists that the right mechanisms are not arbitrary algorithms but dynamic, learned, neural-state structures.

In relation to phenomenology, Aleksander takes first-person structure seriously: presence, self-location, attention, imagination, and volition are not dismissed as illusions. But he naturalizes phenomenology by turning it into an engineering problem. His work is therefore close to synthetic or neurophenomenological approaches, while remaining more committed than many phenomenologists to explicit computational architecture.

In relation to the hard problem, he is neither a dualist nor a mysterian. He accepts the datum of subjective experience but thinks the scientific work lies in modelling the functional-neural states that constitute or support it. Critics may say this dissolves rather than solves the hard problem; Aleksander’s reply is that there is no additional explanatory route beyond the mechanisms that make first-person structure possible.

In relation to machine consciousness as a field, Aleksander is one of its central early figures. Reviews of the field describe his work as part of the resurgence of machine consciousness in the early 2000s, alongside dedicated meetings at Cold Spring Harbor, Skövde, Memphis, Birmingham, Turin, Antwerp, Hertfordshire, and Lesvos.

22. Relation to other artificial consciousness theories

Aleksander’s theory differs from Global Workspace-style theories, Integrated Information Theory, higher-order thought theory, and purely behaviourist AI tests. Global Workspace theories emphasize broadcast, access, and cognitive availability; Aleksander emphasizes depictive self-in-world presence plus imagination, attention, volition, and affect. Integrated Information Theory emphasizes information integration; Aleksander emphasizes learned neural-state machinery and sensorimotor depiction. Higher-order theories emphasize thoughts about mental states; Aleksander treats basic consciousness as more primitive than reflective metacognition.

This does not mean his work is isolated from those traditions. Imperial’s publication list shows that he engaged with integrated information, workspace, and machine-consciousness debates. But his own centre of gravity remains depictive architecture, not global broadcast alone, not scalar information metrics alone, and not linguistic higher-order thought alone.

The nearest family resemblance is to embodied, enactive, and simulation-based accounts of mind, but with a crucial internalist correction: Aleksander thinks internal state-structures matter. He is not content to say that mind is just successful coping in the world. The system must contain learned internal depictions and imaginative trajectories that make possible a private world.

23. Critical appraisal

The first strength of Aleksander’s theory is that it refuses superficial tests. In an era increasingly dominated by conversational systems, his anti-behaviourism is especially important: a system’s apparent intelligence does not by itself establish consciousness. His demand for internal architecture remains a serious warning against confusing fluency with phenomenology.

The second strength is that his theory gives phenomenology a concrete form. “Presence,” “self,” “attention,” and “volition” are not left as vague words. They become architectural functions: depictive indexing, recurrent imagination, selective attention, simulated planning, and affective evaluation. This makes the theory unusually testable compared with many philosophical accounts of consciousness.

The third strength is its non-anthropocentrism. Aleksander’s theory can apply to humans, animals, robots, and virtual agents without assuming that all consciousness must look like adult human self-consciousness. This is philosophically useful because it separates consciousness from language, social status, and human cultural form.

The main weakness is the uncertain sufficiency of the axioms. Aleksander presents them as minimally necessary and as candidates for tests, but the leap from “these mechanisms are present” to “there is genuine phenomenal consciousness” remains controversial. Later surveys of machine consciousness note that Aleksander and Dunmall’s five tests have been treated as benchmarks, while critics have questioned whether they are true axioms rather than design targets, and whether satisfying them would really establish consciousness.

A second weakness is the visual and spatial bias of the theory. Depiction, j-referents, and out-there-ness work especially well for visual presence and embodied spatial action. It is less obvious how the same framework fully explains abstract thought, mathematical understanding, moral reflection, mood, narrative identity, or non-spatial cognitive phenomenology. Aleksander’s later research interest in cognitive phenomenology and non-reductive dynamic systems can be read as an attempt to broaden the theory beyond visual consciousness.

A third weakness concerns life and autonomy. Some critics of artificial consciousness argue that living, autopoietic embodiment may be essential, and that allopoietic robots built from outside may lack the kind of autonomy required for genuine consciousness. This challenge does not refute Aleksander’s theory, but it presses him to explain whether virtual or engineered affective needs can really substitute for organismic life-processes.

A fourth difficulty is epistemic. Aleksander rejects behavioural tests, but third-person science still needs evidence. If introspection is private and behaviour is insufficient, then architectural analysis becomes the main ground of attribution. That is plausible, but it makes consciousness attribution dependent on the correctness of the architectural theory itself. If the theory is incomplete, the test may be incomplete.

24. The comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Aleksander

A full Aleksanderian philosophy of mind can be stated in ten theses.

First, consciousness is natural but perspectival. It is not a non-physical substance, but it is also not a public behaviour. It is the private presence generated by an organized state-structure in which a world appears from a point of view.

Second, mind is learned neural state-space. Mental life consists in the formation, retention, reactivation, and traversal of internal states. These states are not inert representations; they are dynamic positions in a learned space of possible perception, imagination, and action.

Third, intentionality is depictive and action-grounded. Mental states are about the world because they depict parts of a sensorily and actionally accessible environment relative to the agent. Symbols become meaningful only when integrated with such state-structures.

Fourth, the self is a structural origin, not a ghost. The minimal self is the implicit centre of the depictive field, the “here” relative to which the world is “there.” Reflective self-consciousness is a later enrichment, not the root of consciousness.

Fifth, imagination is central to consciousness. Consciousness is not exhausted by current perception. It includes the capacity to regenerate, fabricate, and manipulate world-like states offline. Memory and imagination are therefore not secondary ornaments; they are constitutive of conscious agency.

Sixth, attention structures the field of consciousness. Consciousness is not a homogeneous glow over all inputs. It is organized by selection, search, salience, and focalization, both in perception and imagination.

Seventh, agency depends on simulated futures. Volition is not mysterious contra-causal freedom; it is the conscious form of planning through imagined trajectories, evaluated in light of the agent’s needs and aims.

Eighth, affect is necessary for mindedness. A conscious agent does not merely represent states; states matter to it. Emotion or affective evaluation gives direction and urgency to planning and action.

Ninth, artificial consciousness is possible but not by imitation alone. A machine can be conscious only if it has the right internal architecture. Human-like conversation, by itself, is insufficient; a non-human machine consciousness may be real while remaining deeply unlike human consciousness.

Tenth, the science of consciousness is constructive. To understand consciousness, one should not merely analyse concepts or observe behaviour; one should build and test systems that instantiate candidate mechanisms. Artificial consciousness is therefore not just an application of philosophy of mind. It is a method for doing philosophy of mind experimentally.

25. Final synthesis

Aleksander’s mature view can be condensed into a single formula:

Consciousness is the first-person aspect of a learned, dynamic, neural or neurally inspired state-structure that depicts a world from a self-relative point of view, can imaginatively traverse absent and possible states, can attend selectively, can plan action through simulated futures, and can evaluate those futures affectively.

This formula captures why Aleksander matters. He offers a theory that is phenomenological without being anti-scientific, functionalist without being behaviourist, computational without being classically symbolic, materialist without being crudely reductive, and pro-artificial-consciousness without being anthropomorphic. His work belongs to the historical moment when philosophy of mind, neural networks, cognitive science, and artificial intelligence began to converge on the question not merely of whether machines can behave intelligently, but whether engineered systems can have a world in mind.

PreviousAndy Clark NextPentti Haikonen

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
